title: New dating of ancient Chilean site reopens old wounds
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Why stress is bad for the skin, spiky scales slow bacterial spread, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
23 March 2026
Today’s Deep Dive delves into the debate over Monte Verde and the first people in North America. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including a myth defanged and the benefits of spiky scales.
Health  |  Science
Why stress is bad for the skin
Skin conditions like eczema can have patients worrying about pain, itchiness, and redness. All that worrying seems to actually make the eczema worse, and researchers have finally found why.

A team first assessed the stress levels of 51 eczema patients, finding that those with higher stress levels had worse symptoms, including inflammation. To determine the cause, a team induced eczema in mice, then placed them in one of three stressful situations: a high platform with no guardrails, a tight tube, or a different cage than normal. They also selectively dyed neurons that fire during eczema flares. Indeed, stress caused the neurons to activate immune cells in the skin that caused inflammation and enhanced eczema symptoms. Skin biopsies revealed that stressed mice had four times as many such activated immune cells in their skin as non-stressed mice.

“Integrating mental health interventions, such as stress reduction, cognitive-behavioral therapy, or mindfulness into dermatological care could improve treatment outcomes,” wrote immunologists Nicolas Gaudenzio and Lilian Basso in a related Perspective.

Other treatment possibilities include blocking nerves that respond to stress, or limiting the production of inflammatory molecules by skin immune cells. The new study is “an important piece to the puzzle,” clinical dermatologist Wolfgang Weninger told Nature. “But it needs to be translated into humans as the next step.” 
Read the Science paper
Read the Perspective
Animals  |  Toxins
Once and for all: No, baby snakes aren’t more dangerous than adults
This young Western diamondback looks fierce, but it’s not more dangerous than its parents. © B. P. White via INATURALIST | CC BY-NC
If you grew up in the western United States in the 2000s, you likely heard that you should be especially wary of baby snakes. They’re inexperienced, so when they bite, they dump all their venom at once, leading to a much more dangerous dose despite their small size. Except, that’s not true at all. Study after study has concluded that juvenile snakes are less deadly than their full-grown parents. Still, in a new study, researchers show that for about a decade and a half, this myth was all over the U.S.—and it persists today.

“This is an easily defanged myth that has generated dread, panic, and real-life consequences ,” said toxinologist and lead author William Hayes in a statement. He and his colleague Cale Morris wondered how this patently untrue idea became so pervasive. So, they dug into newspaper archives, finding the first mentions of the excessive danger of young snakes in the late 1960s. The fiction was soon considered fact in California, so much so that in 2009, a survey of first responders found almost three-quarters of them believed it. The story spread from there throughout the United States and remained pervasive until 2015, when scientists began a concerted effort to set the record straight.

“We’re hoping to get the word out so that we can get this myth corrected,” Hayes said. “There’s no need for hikers to have unwarranted fear of baby rattlesnakes or to think they need to harm or kill the snakes.” Indeed, “rattlesnakes occupy an important role in the ecosystems they dwell in and in recent years their populations have dropped significantly in many parts of the United States,” the team explained. “We also don’t want physicians or veterinarians to succumb to pressure from patients and families who insist on excessive medication after a bite from a baby rattlesnake,” Hayes added.
Read the paper
Microbiology  |  News from Science
Spiky scales slow bacterial spread
Ball pythons get their name from a classic defensive maneuver: They coil up into a ball and tuck in their heads. But their scales conceal another, far more subtle form of defense: microscopic spikes that inhibit bacterial buildup. The discovery, reported on 11 March in ACS Omega, could inspire antimicrobial materials that work mechanically rather than chemically.

“We could use mechanics and antibiotics in tandem,” said polymer physicist Andrew Parnell. “We could possibly repurpose some of the old antibiotics in a two-pronged approach.”

Using snakeskins shed from animals at the Plzeň Zoo, researchers mounted individual scales on needles and incubated them in broths rich in nutrients and one of two kinds of bacteria: Escherichia coli or Staphylococcus aureus. After 48 hours, control samples made of a polystyrene plastic were covered in thick, mature biofilms—protective slime layers secreted by microbial communities. But the spiky snake scales were far more resistant to the microbes, with E. coli and S. aureus sticking to them 88% and 78% less, respectively. Under a microscope, the scale surfaces were only sparsely colonized by bacteria that had found refuge between spikes.
Read the full story
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what's possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
Deep Dive
Researchers collected and dated minerals and organic matter from sedimentary layers near the site of the original Monte Verde excavation.  Claudio Latorre
New dating of ancient Chilean site reopens old wounds
It is among the most hotly contested topics in archaeology: When did people first arrive in the Americas? A few decades ago, there was consensus that people began populating North America no earlier than about 13,000 years ago, as evidenced by well-dated spearheads with characteristic fluted bases known as Clovis points, named for the city in New Mexico near the archaeological site where they were first identified in 1929. Then, in the 1970s, an archaeologist named Tom Dillehay claimed to have dated stone tools and other artifacts found at a site in southern Chile to 14,500 years ago—a time when much of North America would have still be covered in impassable glaciers.

Few gave the claims from that site, known as Monte Verde, much credence until an independent cadre of researchers visited it in 1997 and confirmed the dates . The result sent shockwaves through the archaeological community and overturned the so-called “Clovis first” paradigm in favor of a new “pre-Clovis” one. This interpretation spurred scientists to retool their hypotheses for how and when people entered the continent; a coastal migration hypothesis has since gained popularity in its wake. Several other sites throughout the Americas have since been dated to before 13,000 years, and the matter appeared settled.

But old ideas die hard in this field, and prominent “Clovis first” supporters have continued to express doubt in “pre-Clovis” findings. A study published last week in Science saw one such “pre-Clovis” skeptic revisiting Monte Verde—or sites close to it, as the original dig site has since been destroyed—and redating the sediment layers from which the supposedly humanmade artifacts were said to have come. “ This is going to cause a furor in the field ,” said Stuart Fiedel, a retired archaeologist and longtime critic of pre-Clovis sites, including Monte Verde. “There’s going to be a fight.”

In the authors’ analysis, the key layers aren’t 14,500 years old, but instead only 8200 to 4200 years old. The confusion, they argued in the paper, was caused by younger sedimentary material being deposited below older layers by a river’s flow, which scrambled the region’s stratigraphy.

Why did it take so long to uncover this? “The simple answer is that the archaeological site is quite remote and was not visited by many archeologists, geomorphologists, or Quaternary geologists (studying the past 2.6 million years) who could have identified the prominent volcanic ash, the exposed wood bed that is the source for the 14,000-year-old radiocarbon ages, and identified that the site is situated on an abandoned floodplain that had to be younger than the landscape surface,” explained Jason Rech in a related Perspective.

As Fiedel predicted, “pre-Clovis” supporters lined up to challenge the findings, while old “Clovis first” skeptics defended them. Critically, these dates from Monte Verde have no impact on the other pre-Clovis sites that have been independently dated, but the brouhaha could lead to enhanced scrutiny and calls for their dates to be replicated.

Such efforts would raise new questions, said Rech. “Will these sites survive critical review by other scientists? Moreover, will archaeologists be open-minded enough to accept these sites if both the evidence for human occupation and age control is robust?”
Read the Science paper and PERSPECTIVE  |  Listen to the  Related Podcast
Read the full story
Et Cetera
Moon not so hot-and-cold
The first results from the Blue Ghost robotic lander, reported this week at the Lunar and Planetary Science Conference in Texas, are shaking up a view that the Moon is neatly divided into a hot spot on its near side, with cooler terrain everywhere else, says Seiichi Nagihara, a geophysicist and principal investigator of Blue Ghost’s heat probe instrument. “We may have to abandon that binary.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
Merging physics
The largest U.S. funder of the physical sciences—the Department of Energy’s Office of Science—will merge its high energy physics and nuclear physics programs. Many researchers worry the move may signal a marginalization of both fields. “The community as a whole is very worried,” said one nuclear physicist.
Read more at scienceInsider
Cigarettes are for the birds
All over the world, birds are stuffing cigarette butts into their nests. Are these aves addicted to nicotine? More likely, researchers report, they’re using them as a primitive form of fumigation, with toxins in tobacco helping rid their nests of dangerous parasites. “Birds are smart,” one evolutionary ecologist said.
Animal Behaviour Paper  |  Read more at The New York Times
I didn’t think I had a chance … You break all the glass ceilings together and still they look at you eventually as [just] being an Arab.
—Mouna Maroun, University of Haifa
NEWS FROM SCIENCE  |  19 March 2026  |  Eli Kintisch
The Arab neuroscientist was surprised by the faculty’s vote to elect her as rector in 2024, just months after deadly conflict in Gaza broke out. She’s now steering the Israeli university through tumultuous times, and has learned some hard lessons.
Last but not least
I’ve become especially partial to fungi after living so many years in the Pacific Northwest. We have so much to learn about these long-overlooked organisms; I’m especially excited for more work on how they might help us survive the devastating effects of climate change, like an increasingly fiery world.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Hannah Richter, Eric Hand, and Michael Price
